verses.text: Then all the children of Israel went out, and the congregation was gathered together as one man, from Dan even to Beersheba, with the land of Gilead, unto the LORD in Mizpeh. The chief of all the people, even of all the tribes of Israel, presented themselves in the assembly of the people of God, four hundred thousand footmen that drew sword. (Now the children of Benjamin heard that the children of Israel were gone up to Mizpeh.) Then said the children of Israel, Tell us, how was this wickedness? Guidance: For the first time in the book of Judges, all twelve tribes come together — 'from Dan to Beersheba.' The phrase that should mark covenant unity finally appears in the worst possible context: to investigate an atrocity. 400,000 swordsmen gather 'unto the LORD in Mizpeh.' The nation that could not unite to fight Canaanites unites to discipline its own. The instinct is right; the chapter's tragedy is what happens when an angry, righteous nation forgets to ask God before it acts.

Guidance: For the first time in the book of Judges, all twelve tribes come together — 'from Dan to Beersheba.' The phrase that should mark covenant unity finally appears in the worst possible context: to investigate an atrocity. 400,000 swordsmen gather 'unto the LORD in Mizpeh.' The Levite, the husband of the woman that was slain, answered and said, I came into Gibeah that belongeth to Benjamin, I and my concubine, to lodge. The men of Gibeah rose against me, and beset the house round about upon me by night, and thought to have slain me: and my concubine have they forced, that she is dead. I took my concubine, and cut her in pieces, and sent her throughout all the country of the inheritance of Israel: for they have committed lewdness and folly in Israel. Behold, ye are all children of Israel; give here your advice and counsel. Guidance: The Levite testifies. Note what he omits: he does not mention that he pushed his concubine out the door. He says they 'thought to have slain me' (probably true) and that the men 'forced her, that she is dead' (also true). But the personal cowardice is edited out. Testimony often shapes itself into a version that protects the witness. Israel acts on partial information — and the next forty-five verses are what happens when righteous outrage is built on incomplete confession.

All the people arose as one man, saying, We will not any of us go to his tent, neither will we any of us turn into his house. But now this shall be the thing which we will do to Gibeah; we will go up by lot against it; and we will take ten men of an hundred throughout all the tribes of Israel, and an hundred of a thousand, and a thousand out of ten thousand, to fetch victual for the people, that they may do, when they come to Gibeah of Benjamin, according to all the folly that they have wrought in Israel. So all the men of Israel were gathered against the city, knit together as one man. Guidance: 'As one man' — the chapter's catchphrase. Israel unites with logistical care: one in ten will gather provisions while nine in ten fight. They are organized, disciplined, righteous-minded. And yet they have not asked the LORD whether to fight, only how to win. The 'as one man' will turn out to be a mass that the LORD will let stumble until it learns to listen instead of plan.

The tribes of Israel sent men through all the tribe of Benjamin, saying, What wickedness is this that is done among you? Now therefore deliver us the men, the children of Belial, which are in Gibeah, that we may put them to death, and put away evil from Israel. But the children of Benjamin would not hearken to the voice of their brethren the children of Israel. Guidance: A formal, lawful demand: hand over the criminals. Deut 13 and 17 commanded exactly this kind of accountability. Benjamin's refusal is theologically lethal — they protect a city of Belial-sons rather than purge it. Tribal loyalty over covenant loyalty. The smallest tribe stakes its very existence on shielding its worst men. Every refusal to discipline the obvious wrong inside one's own community is the same calculation Benjamin is making here, in miniature.

The children of Benjamin gathered themselves together out of the cities unto Gibeah, to go out to battle against the children of Israel. The children of Benjamin were numbered at that time out of the cities twenty and six thousand men that drew sword, beside the inhabitants of Gibeah, which were numbered seven hundred chosen men. Among all this people there were seven hundred chosen men lefthanded; every one could sling stones at an hair breadth, and not miss. All the children of Israel, beside Benjamin, were numbered four hundred thousand men that drew sword: all these were men of war. Guidance: 27,000 against 400,000 — fifteen-to-one odds. Benjamin's seven hundred left-handed slingers are legendary marksmen — 'sling stones at a hair-breadth and not miss' — but it should not be enough to win. The tribe's whole strategy is to bet on quality over numbers. The narrative is setting up its surprise: God will let Benjamin win the first two rounds despite the odds. Sometimes He does not give the righteous side easy victories; sometimes He humbles them first.

The children of Israel arose, and went up to the house of God, and asked counsel of God, and said, Which of us shall go up first to the battle against the children of Benjamin? And the LORD said, Judah shall go up first. The children of Israel rose up in the morning, and encamped against Gibeah. The men of Israel went out to battle against Benjamin; and the men of Israel put themselves in array to fight against them at Gibeah. The children of Benjamin came forth out of Gibeah, and destroyed down to the ground of the Israelites that day twenty and two thousand men. Guidance: Israel does ask the LORD — but only about marching order, not about the war itself. Same mistake as Joshua 9 (the Gibeonite treaty) — partial inquiry. God answers 'Judah first' (same answer as Judg 1:2) but does not promise victory. Israel hears the order and assumes the result. Twenty-two thousand fall in one day. The numbers are catastrophic for the larger army; Benjamin's slingers do exactly what was advertised. The chapter is showing what asking the right questions in the wrong depth looks like.

The people the men of Israel encouraged themselves, and set their battle again in array in the place where they put themselves in array the first day. The children of Israel went up and wept before the LORD until even, and asked counsel of the LORD, saying, Shall I go up again to battle against the children of Benjamin my brother? And the LORD said, Go up against him. Guidance: First, they encourage themselves ('the men of Israel'). Then they weep before the LORD. The order matters: human resolve first, divine inquiry second. They ask a slightly better question this time — 'shall I go up again?' instead of 'who goes first?' God's answer is again terse: 'Go up.' Notice He still does not promise victory. He has only said yes to the going. Israel is going to find out that 'go up' and 'win' are not always the same word.

The people the men of Israel encouraged themselves, and set their battle again in array in the place where they put themselves in array the first day. The children of Israel went up and wept before the LORD until even, and asked counsel of the LORD, saying, Shall I go up again to battle against the children of Benjamin my brother? And the LORD said, Go up against him. Guidance: First, they encourage themselves ('the men of Israel'). Then they weep before the LORD. The order matters: human resolve first, divine inquiry second. They ask a slightly better question this time — 'shall I go up again?' instead of 'who goes first?' God's answer is again terse: 'Go up.' Notice He still does not promise victory. He has only said yes to the going. Israel is going to find out that 'go up' and 'win' are not always the same word.

The children of Israel came near against the children of Benjamin the second day. Benjamin went forth against them out of Gibeah the second day, and destroyed down to the ground of the children of Israel again eighteen thousand men; all these drew the sword. Guidance: Eighteen thousand more dead. Two days; forty thousand Israelites killed by their own brothers. The chapter's silence about whose 'side' God is on is louder than any commentary. Both armies are 'as one man'; both have been wicked in their own way. The Levite who started it is alive. The army that was right to be furious is hemorrhaging on the field. Right cause does not guarantee easy outcome.

The children of Israel came near against the children of Benjamin the second day. Guidance: Eighteen thousand more dead. Two days; forty thousand Israelites killed by their own brothers. The chapter's silence about whose 'side' God is on is louder than any commentary. Both armies are 'as one man'; both have been wicked in their own way. The Levite who started it is alive. The army that was right to be furious is hemorrhaging on the field. Right cause does not guarantee easy outcome.

Then all the children of Israel, and all the people, went up, and came unto the house of God, and wept, and sat there before the LORD, and fasted that day until even, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before the LORD. The children of Israel enquired of the LORD, (for the ark of the covenant of God was there in those days, and Phinehas, the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron, stood before it in those days,) saying, Shall I yet again go out to battle against the children of Benjamin my brother, or shall I cease? And the LORD said, Go up; for to morrow I will deliver them into thine hand. Guidance: Now Israel weeps, fasts, and offers sacrifices — repentance posture. The narrator drops a quiet detail: Phinehas, Aaron's grandson, is the priest. We are very early in the post-Joshua era. The mention dates the civil war (probably while the conquest generation's grandchildren were still alive). And this time the prayer is fully covenant-shaped — 'or shall I cease?' offers God the option of stopping the war. They are finally yielded to the answer rather than the action. And now God adds the promise: 'tomorrow I will deliver them into thine hand.' Two days of grief unlocked the word that one day of rage could not.

Israel set liers in wait round about Gibeah. The children of Israel went up against the children of Benjamin on the third day, and put themselves in array against Gibeah, as at other times. The children of Benjamin went out against the people, and were drawn away from the city; and they began to smite of the people, and kill, as at other times, in the highways, of which one goeth up to the house of God, and the other to Gibeah in the field, about thirty men of Israel. The children of Benjamin said, They are smitten down before us, as at the first. But the children of Israel said, Let us flee, and draw them from the city unto the highways. All the men of Israel rose up out of their place, and put themselves in array at Baal-tamar: and the liers in wait of Israel came forth out of their places, even out of the meadows of Gibeah. There came against Gibeah ten thousand chosen men out of all Israel, and the battle was sore: but they knew not that evil was near them. The LORD smote Benjamin before Israel: and the children of Israel destroyed of the Benjamites that day twenty and five thousand and an hundred men: all these drew the sword. Guidance: Israel borrows the Ai playbook from Joshua 8 — feigned retreat plus rear ambush. Benjamin sees what looks like a repeat of the past two days and grins ('as at the first') — pride blinds them to the new variable, the Spirit-given strategy. 'They knew not that evil was near them.' Twenty-five thousand one hundred fall. The narrator credits the win precisely: 'The LORD smote Benjamin before Israel.' Now the verb God had withheld is finally given. He waited for the right army to win the right way.

The LORD smote Benjamin before Israel: and the children of Israel destroyed of the Benjamites that day twenty and five thousand and an hundred men: all these drew the sword. Guidance: Israel borrows the Ai playbook from Joshua 8 — feigned retreat plus rear ambush. Benjamin sees what looks like a repeat of the past two days and grins ('as at the first') — pride blinds them to the new variable, the Spirit-given strategy. 'They knew not that evil was near them.' Twenty-five thousand one hundred fall. The narrator credits the win precisely: 'The LORD smote Benjamin before Israel.' Now the verb God had withheld is finally given. He waited for the right army to win the right way.

Israel set liers in wait round about Gibeah. The children of Israel went up against the children of Benjamin on the third day, and put themselves in array against Gibeah, as at other times. The children of Benjamin went out against the people, and were drawn away from the city; and they began to smite of the people, and kill, as at other times, in the highways, of which one goeth up to the house of God, and the other to Gibeah in the field, about thirty men of Israel. The children of Benjamin said, They are smitten down before us, as at the first. But the children of Israel said, Let us flee, and draw them from the city unto the highways. All the men of Israel rose up out of their place, and put themselves in array at Baal-tamar: and the liers in wait of Israel came forth out of their places, even out of the meadows of Gibeah. There came against Gibeah ten thousand chosen men out of all Israel, and the battle was sore: but they knew not that evil was near them. The LORD smote Benjamin before Israel: and the children of Israel destroyed of the Benjamites that day twenty and five thousand and an hundred men: all these drew the sword. So the children of Benjamin saw that they were smitten: for the men of Israel gave place to the Benjamites, because they trusted unto the liers in wait which they had set beside Gibeah. The liers in wait hasted, and rushed upon Gibeah; and the liers in wait drew themselves along, and smote all the city with the edge of the sword. Now there was an appointed sign between the men of Israel and the liers in wait, that they should make a great flame with smoke rise up out of the city. When the men of Israel retired in the battle, Benjamin began to smite and kill of the men of Israel about thirty persons: for they said, Surely they are smitten down before us, as in the first battle. But when the flame began to arise up out of the city with a pillar of smoke, the Benjamites looked behind them, and, behold, the flame of the city ascended up to heaven. When the men of Israel turned again, the men of Benjamin were amazed: for they saw that evil was come upon them. Therefore they turned their backs before the men of Israel unto the way of the wilderness; but the battle overtook them; and them which came out of the cities they destroyed in the midst of them. Guidance: A film-script of the trap, told in slow motion. The smoke signal rises; Benjamin looks back; their city is on fire. The Hebrew word for 'amazed' is the same used of Pharaoh's army at the Red Sea. In a moment, Benjamin transitions from triumphant pursuers to panicked refugees. The narrator's verdict: 'they saw that evil was come upon them.' What God's people had been carrying for two days lands on Benjamin in an afternoon.

Therefore they turned their backs before the men of Israel unto the way of the wilderness; but the battle overtook them; and them which came out of the cities they destroyed in the midst of them. Thus they inclosed the Benjamites round about, and chased them, and trode them down with ease over against Gibeah toward the sunrising. There fell of Benjamin eighteen thousand men; all these were men of valour. They turned and fled toward the wilderness unto the rock of Rimmon: and they gleaned of them in the highways five thousand men; and pursued hard after them unto Gidom, and slew two thousand men of them. So that all which fell that day of Benjamin were twenty and five thousand men that drew the sword; all these were men of valour. Guidance: The narrative narrows. 18,000 fall in the main battle, 5,000 along the roads, 2,000 near Gidom. The chapter records the math because the math is the tragedy. Twenty-five thousand men of valor of one tribe — close to the tribe's entire fighting strength — die in a day. Compare the conquest battles where God killed the enemy; here His people kill each other. The chapter's verbs are the same; only the bodies are kin. Civil war is the LORD's most painful judgment because both sides bury sons.

But six hundred men turned and fled to the wilderness unto the rock Rimmon, and abode in the rock Rimmon four months. The men of Israel turned again upon the children of Benjamin, and smote them with the edge of the sword, as well the men of every city, as the beast, and all that came to hand: also they set on fire all the cities that they came to. Guidance: Six hundred Benjamites — the entire remnant of the tribe — escape to a desert refuge for four months. Israel then turns on the cities themselves: men, beasts, structures. The numbers are the book's grimmest math: a tribe of God's covenant people goes from 27,000 fighting men to 600 over a single day's battle and the cleanup that follows. The next chapter will be Israel's panicked attempt to rebuild the tribe they have just nearly erased. The cost of waiting too long to address evil is exactly here: when the discipline finally lands, it lands like a wrecking ball. The chapter is not a celebration; it is a tragedy. The book of Judges has reached its lowest point — a holocaust within the covenant family — and there is no king to prevent it from happening again.
